notes.note: The heading "Cicero to Servius, greetings." The recipient is Servius Sulpicius Rufus (cos. 51 BC), the great jurist, who served as proconsul of Achaea 46--45 BC; he died on embassy in 43 BC and received a public funeral lauded by Cicero in the Ninth Philippic.

notes.anchor: whom I saw beside himself with delight
notes.note: T. Pomponius Atticus, Cicero's closest friend and financial adviser, was a major property-owner in Epirus; Servius had evidently written to Atticus directly, an unsolicited gesture that delighted both men.

notes.anchor: in the affairs of Epirus and the rest
notes.note: Atticus derived a large portion of his income from his estates in Epirus (north-west Greece), which he had inherited from an uncle and developed with slave-run flocks; Servius, as proconsul of Achaea, had jurisdiction over or proximity to these holdings and could offer practical protection.

notes.anchor: by whatever good offices, in the affairs of Epirus and the rest, you bind Atticus to yourself, by those same offices you will lay me under obligation to you
notes.note: A characteristic Ciceronian formula: benefactions to a friend are treated as debts owed to the friend's patron (Cicero). The letter thus converts Servius's courtesy to Atticus into a personal obligation on Cicero's own credit.
